old_claim.note: Harbor Lighthouses provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency is retained because maritime pilotage can use landmarks, periploi, local knowledge, and sailing directions without harbor lighthouses.

source_shape.source_locator: Development of marine navigation states that early navigators used landmarks; pilot books/periploi survive from the 4th century BCE and described routes, headlands, landmarks, anchorages, currents, and port entrances.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Britannica describes coastal piloting and pilot books as navigation practices based on landmarks and sailing instructions, independent of lighthouse infrastructure.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Harbor lighthouses are aids for some harbors, but local pilotage is a broader practice built from route and hazard knowledge and should not require lighthouses.

ontology_before: Maritime pilotage was modeled as depending on harbor lighthouses, making a broad navigation practice depend on one later aid.
ontology_after: Maritime pilotage remains connected to navigation and sailing, while lighthouses remain a separate harbor-safety technology.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant changed: Maritime pilotage must not directly depend on harbor lighthouses.
would_reject_if: A source shows this node is scoped only to lighthouse-assisted harbor pilotage.
The graph splits broad maritime pilotage from harbor approach signaling and keeps this node for the latter only.
A source demonstrates that historical pilotage could not function without harbor lighthouses.
short_reason: Pilotage is broader than and historically independent of harbor lighthouses.
